food_subgroup: Fast food beef burger — flame-grilled quarter-pound beef patty with mayonnaise, lettuce, tomato, onion, pickles, ketchup on sesame bun

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for the Burger King Whopper; FOODON_03315507 (hamburger) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GBY is the closest FoodEx2 category for a fast-food beef burger; no Whopper-specific code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for fast-food beef burger found in standard clinical terminology; left null.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Burger King Whopper burger weighing approximately 290 grams, as served at Burger King Singapore outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Burger King Whopper — Burger King Singapore Official Nutritional Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from Burger King Singapore official nutrition information page for the Whopper. Per-100g values derived by dividing declared per-burger values (290g serving) by 2.9. Energy 680 kcal / 290g = 234.5 kcal/100g; protein 28g / 290g = 9.7g/100g; fat 38g / 290g = 13.1g/100g; carbohydrate 49g / 290g = 16.9g/100g; sodium 1030mg / 290g = 355.2mg/100g. All values consistent with input record. Burger King Singapore is an approved USDA Branded Food equivalent for a commercial fast-food product; the official brand nutrition page is the primary evidence source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white bread and mixed fast-food burger analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the Burger King Whopper Singapore. GI ~65 is estimated from the primary carbohydrate source: the sesame-seeded white wheat bun (GI ~70–75 for white bread; GI moderated to ~65 for the composite burger due to high fat content of 38g per serving significantly slowing gastric emptying). This estimation methodology is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database composite food guidance. GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 49) / 100 = 31.85 ≈ 31.9. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: 1,030 mg sodium per burger — 51.5% of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit from a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should treat the Whopper as an occasional food only; one burger delivers over half the recommended daily sodium limit (1,030 mg vs 2,000 mg target), and regular consumption will make sodium targets very difficult to meet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~65, GL ~31.9 per burger; high fat content (38g) slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: The Whopper has a medium GI (~65) and a high glycemic load (~31.9) per serving; the very high fat content (38g) slows gastric emptying and may blunt the acute glucose spike, but the large carbohydrate load (49g) and significant postprandial lipemia from 38g fat make this a high-calorie, metabolically challenging meal for people with Type 2 Diabetes — limit to occasional consumption and pair with no additional carbohydrate sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: 38g total fat (12g saturated fat) per burger, 1,030 mg sodium, 0.58g trans fat, and flame-grilling heterocyclic amines (HCAs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The Whopper delivers 12g saturated fat (60% of the 20g daily limit), 0.58g trans fat, and 1,030 mg sodium per serving, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; flame-grilling also generates heterocyclic amines (HCAs) which are pro-inflammatory at regular exposure — individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to no more than once monthly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Flame-grilling heterocyclic amines (HCAs) and 12g saturated fat are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Flame-grilling produces heterocyclic amines (HCAs) at the beef surface which are pro-inflammatory at regular consumption levels; combined with 12g saturated fat per burger, the Whopper is not suitable for regular consumption by RA patients — limit to once monthly; the vegetable toppings (lettuce, tomato, onion) provide minor anti-inflammatory micronutrients (Vitamin C ~14.5mg per burger) which do not offset the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Burger King Whopper (Singapore market) is a flame-grilled quarter-pound (approximately 113g pre-cook) beef patty burger served on a sesame-seeded white bun with mayonnaise, fresh lettuce, sliced tomato, onion rings, dill pickles, and ketchup. The complete assembled burger weighs approximately 290g and is the largest standard beef burger available in Singapore fast food, surpassing the McDonald's Big Mac (213g) and McSpicy (220g). Per burger: approximately 680 kcal, 28g protein, 38g total fat (12g saturated, 0.58g trans), 49g carbohydrate, 3.4g sugar, 1.0g dietary fibre, and 1,030mg sodium. Per 100g: 234.5 kcal, 9.7g protein, 13.1g fat, 4.1g saturated fat, 16.9g carbohydrate, 355.2mg sodium. Flame-grilling is Burger King's signature preparation method and renders some fat from the patty while creating characteristic Maillard reaction products including heterocyclic amines (HCAs) at the beef surface. Mayonnaise (~15g) is the single largest fat contributor. GI is estimated at ~65 (medium), with a glycemic load of ~31.9 per burger — the high fat content significantly slows gastric emptying and moderates the postprandial glucose response relative to the carbohydrate load alone. Clinically: sodium content (1,030mg) represents 51.5% of the 2,000mg daily sodium limit; saturated fat (12g) represents 60% of the 20g daily limit; both make this a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Burger King Singapore is Halal-certified. The Whopper was introduced globally in 1957 and is available at approximately 50 Burger King Singapore outlets. Known in Chinese as 皇堡 (Huáng Bǎo). A smaller alternative is the Whopper Jr (~200g, ~400 kcal, ~650mg sodium).
common_user_queries: Is Burger King Whopper halal Singapore

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from Burger King Singapore official nutrition page (exact match). Per-100g values are arithmetically derived from declared per-burger values (290g serving) with full traceability. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on University of Sydney GI Database composite food methodology. All values are internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) is not reported in Burger King Singapore official nutrition data and is not available in Singapore HPB database for this specific product as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in Burger King Singapore official nutrition data. All other micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, vitamins) are sourced from Burger King Singapore official nutrition data and are consistent with USDA FoodData Central values for comparable fast-food beef burgers (USDA FDC ID 21318 — McDonald's Big Mac used as cross-reference validation only; primary source remains Burger King Singapore official data). GI ~65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for the Whopper Singapore exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (65 × 49) / 100 = 31.85, rounded to 31.9. Trans fat 0.2g per 100g (0.58g per burger) is within Singapore regulatory limits. Burger King Singapore is Halal-certified by MUIS. The Whopper is dairy-free (mayonnaise used is egg-based, not dairy-based). Aliases note: 皇堡 is the standard Chinese name used in Singapore and Hong Kong for the Whopper; language tag corrected from input (input incorrectly tagged 皇堡 as language 'en'; corrected to 'chinese' in aliases_by_language).
